conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I need to gather some information about your circumstances. Let me start by asking:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children I'd like to ask about. There's Alex, who's 17, and Blake, who's just 3 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you assess eligibility for both Alex (17) and Blake (3).

Let me start gathering the necessary information. First:

**Do both Alex and Blake live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live with me at home. We're in Bristol.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to ask:

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake? By this I mean, do you look after them and pay towards their upbringing (for example, food, clothes, housing, etc.)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I take care of both of them and cover all their day-to-day needs - food, clothes, school supplies, everything really.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Now I need to ask about your residency status:

**Do you live in the UK, and are you a British citizen, or do you have the right to live and work in the UK (for example, through settled status, indefinite leave to remain, or another immigration status)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Bristol as I mentioned. And yes, I'm a British citizen.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Now I need to ask about Alex specifically, as they are 17:

**Is Alex currently in full-time, non-advanced education or approved training? For example, is Alex still in school, sixth form, or college doing A-levels, GCSEs, or similar qualifications (not university)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in education at the moment. They finished school but haven't gone on to sixth form or college or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me just check one more thing about Alex:

**Has Alex left full-time education or training? If so, when did they leave - was it before or after their 16th birthday, and roughly when did this happen?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex has left education. They left after turning 16 - finished up at the end of Year 11 when they were 16 and decided not to continue on to further study.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me now read the official guidance to ensure I give you an accurate assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I've reviewed the official guidance and now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary

Based on the information you've provided and the official Child Benefit rules:

**For Blake (age 3): ✓ ELIGIBLE**
You are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. They meet all the criteria - they're under 16, live with you, and you're responsible for their care.

**For Alex (age 17): ✗ NOT ELIGIBLE**
Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. While Child Benefit can continue for young people up to age 20, they must be in approved education or training. Since Alex left education after Year 11 and is not currently studying or in training, they don't meet this requirement.

**Important note:** If Alex had registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services when they left education, you could have claimed Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks after they left. If Alex decides to return to full-time education or approved training in the future, you may be able to claim Child Benefit for them again at that time.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has left full-time education after turning 16. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, OR if the child is 16-19 years old and stays in approved education or training. Since Alex left education after Year 11 and is not currently in approved education or training, they do not meet the criteria for Child Benefit. The benefit would have stopped when Alex left education (though there is a 20-week extension period available if they registered with a government-sponsored careers service or armed services, but this was not mentioned).

Blake is 3 years old, lives with you, and you are responsible for their care and upbringing. You are a British citizen living in the UK. Blake meets all the eligibility criteria: they are under 16 years old, they live with you, and you are responsible for them (paying for food, clothes, and their day-to-day needs). Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a British citizen living in Bristol with two children. They are responsible for both children who live with them. Blake (age 3) meets all eligibility criteria as they are under 16. However, Alex (age 17) does not qualify because they have left full-time education and are not in approved education or training. Child Benefit eligibility for children aged 16-19 requires them to stay in approved education or training, which Alex is not doing.

According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, OR if the child is 16-19 years old and stays in approved education or training. Since Alex left education after Year 11 and is not currently in approved education or training, they do not meet the criteria for Child Benefit. The benefit would have stopped when Alex left education (though there is a 20-week extension period available if they registered with a government-sponsored careers service or armed services, but this was not mentioned).

Blake is 3 years old, lives with you, and you are responsible for their care and upbringing. You are a British citizen living in the UK. Blake meets all the eligibility criteria: they are under 16 years old, they live with you, and you are responsible for them (paying for food, clothes, and their day-to-day needs). Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a British citizen living in Bristol with two children. They are responsible for both children who live with them. Blake (age 3) meets all eligibility criteria as they are under 16. However, Alex (age 17) does not qualify because they have left full-time education and are not in approved education or training. Child Benefit eligibility for children aged 16-19 requires them to stay in approved education or training, which Alex is not doing.
